title: Russian Roulette

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: A six-chamber revolver is loaded with bullets in some configuration. You and an opponent take turns pulling the trigger.
rules: There are 4 different scenarios -- each addressed in a separate stage.
Optimal strategy depends on whether the barrel is respun, how many bullets, and whether they're consecutive.
setup.question: In each scenario, what's the optimal action?

learningObjectives: Recognize that 'spinning the barrel' resets information; 'no spin' preserves conditional information.
Compare P(die) with vs without spin to choose the right action.
For Part 4: condition on the opponent surviving to update which chambers remain.

stages.label: Part 1: 1 bullet, no respinning, you go first
stages.prompt: 1 bullet in a 6-chamber revolver, barrel spun ONCE at the start. Two players alternate trigger pulls (you first). What's P(you lose)?

options.text: P = 3/6 = 1/2.  The bullet is equally likely to be in any of 6 chambers; you fire chambers 1, 3, 5 (odd positions). Three of six positions are yours -- 50/50.

stages.explanation: Without re-spinning, chambers 1, 3, 5 are yours and 2, 4, 6 are the opponent's. Bullet placement is uniform, so each player's odds are exactly 3/6 = 1/2.

stages.label: Part 2: 1 bullet, RESPUN before each pull, you go first
stages.prompt: Same revolver, but now the barrel is RE-SPUN before each pull. Each shot is independent: P(fire = bullet) = 1/6 each turn. What's P(you lose)?

options.text: Set up p = (1/6)*1 + (5/6)*(1 - p), giving 11p/6 = 1, so p = 6/11.  Choose to be SECOND to flip the disadvantage.

stages.explanation: First player loses with prob 1/6 immediately. Otherwise (prob 5/6), they survive and the opponent now becomes the 'first', so first player loses with prob (1 - p). Solve: p = 1/6 + (5/6)(1 - p), giving p = 6/11. Going SECOND gives 5/11 -- favored.

stages.label: Part 3: 2 bullets, you can choose to spin or not
stages.prompt: Now there are 2 bullets (random positions) in the 6-chamber revolver. The barrel was spun, an empty chamber fired. Now it's your turn -- spin or not?

options.text: Compare: P(die | spin) = 2/6 = 1/3.  P(die | no spin) = 2/5 (only 5 chambers left, 2 still loaded).  Since 1/3 < 2/5, SPIN.

stages.explanation: Two bullets uniformly placed. After one empty fires (no spin), 5 chambers remain with 2 bullets, P(next is bullet) = 2/5 = 0.4. Re-spinning resets to P = 2/6 = 0.333. Spinning is safer.

stages.label: Part 4: 2 CONSECUTIVE bullets, opponent survived
stages.prompt: Same 2 bullets but now in CONSECUTIVE chambers (say 5 and 6). Barrel was spun; opponent's chamber fired empty. Should YOU spin or not?

options.text: Conditional on opponent surviving: their chamber was 1, 2, 3, or 4 (each prob 1/4).  Your next chamber: k=1->2, k=2->3, k=3->4 (all empty), k=4->5 (bullet).  P(survive | no spin) = 3/4.  P(survive | spin) = 4/6 = 2/3.  Since 3/4 > 2/3, DO NOT spin.

stages.explanation: Knowing the bullets are CONSECUTIVE and the opponent survived narrows where they are. The next chamber after their empty is bullet only in 1 of 4 cases (they had chamber 4 -> next is 5 = bullet). Survival 3/4 > 2/3 (the spin alternative). Don't spin.

stages.label: Commit: meta-lesson
stages.prompt: What's the meta-lesson across these four parts?

options.text: Spin when you have UNFAVORABLE conditional information; don't spin when you have FAVORABLE conditional information. Compare conditional vs unconditional probabilities.

stages.explanation: The decision rule is: P(die | no spin, given everything you know) vs P(die | spin) = bullets/6. Pick whichever is smaller. Spinning RESETS information; sometimes you want to keep favorable information (Part 4), sometimes you want to escape unfavorable information (Part 3).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Generalize Part 2: with k bullets in 6 chambers, respun, what's P(you lose) as the first player?
For Part 4, what if the bullets were in DISJOINT (non-consecutive) chambers? Recompute.
Why does going SECOND in Part 2 give a strict advantage but the players are SYMMETRIC in Part 1?
